name: Relations and Functions — Machines that Map
whatIsIt: A RELATION links members of one set to another (a set of ordered pairs). A FUNCTION is a special relation where every input has EXACTLY ONE output — like a machine: put x in, get f(x) out, and the same x never gives two different answers. The set of allowed inputs is the DOMAIN; the set of outputs is the RANGE.
whyNeeded: Functions are the language of all higher maths and science: distance as a function of time, cost as a function of quantity, temperature as a function of hour. Knowing domain, range and the 'one-output' rule lets you model and predict real relationships precisely.
realLifeUses: Cost as a function of how many items you buy
Distance travelled as a function of time
A vending machine: each button (input) gives one snack (output)
Converting temperature from Celsius to Fahrenheit

vocabulary.meaning: Any set of ordered pairs (x, y) linking two sets.
A relation where each input x has exactly one output f(x).
The set of all allowed inputs (x-values).
The set of all outputs (y-values) the function actually produces.

story.title: The Juice Machine
story.text: Uncle's juice machine gives one glass for every 2 oranges: 1 glass for 2, 2 glasses for 4, 3 for 6. Anaya writes it as f(x) = x ÷ 2. 'If I put in 10 oranges?' she asks. f(10) = 10 ÷ 2 = 5 glasses. Uncle nods: 'Each amount of oranges gives exactly one number of glasses — that makes it a function, not just a relation.'
story.answerInStory: 5 — the machine turns 10 oranges into 10 ÷ 2 = 5 glasses.

props.label: input x → f(x): each input maps to exactly one output

visual.caption: A function is a machine: input x on the left, output f(x) on the right — one input never gives two outputs.

teachingGallery.title: One input, one output
teachingGallery.note: If an input maps to two different outputs, it is NOT a function.

examples.caption: Each input gives exactly one output — a function.
Domain is the inputs; range is the outputs produced.
A relation is just a set of (x, y) pairs; a function adds the one-output rule.

standardMethod.summary: Check each input has one output (function test). To evaluate, substitute the input for x in f(x). Domain = allowed inputs; range = the outputs you get.
steps: List the ordered pairs or the rule f(x).
Function test: does any input appear with two different outputs? If yes, it is not a function.
To find f(a), replace every x in the rule with a and simplify.
Domain = set of inputs; range = set of outputs.
Check: outputs should be single and well-defined for every allowed input.
So it is NOT a function.

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling a relation a function when one input maps to two outputs.
commonMistakes.fix: A function allows each input only ONE output. If x = 1 gives both 2 and 5, it is a relation but not a function.
Domain = the INPUTS (x-values you put in). Range = the OUTPUTS (y-values you get out).

easy.q: For f(x) = x + 2, what is f(5)?
The relation {(1,4),(2,5),(3,6)} has how many pairs in its domain?

questions.q: The range of f(x)=2x for inputs {1,2,3} — how many output values?

hintLadder: Each input must give one output.
The first one is the function.

teachBackPrompt: Describe a 'machine' like f(x) = 2x to a grown-up: show that putting in 3 gives 6 and each input has exactly one output. Explain the domain (inputs) and range (outputs), and why a rule that gives two answers for one input is not a function.

revisionCard.summary: Relation = set of (x, y) pairs. Function = each input has EXACTLY ONE output f(x). Domain = inputs; range = outputs. Evaluate by substituting: f(3) means put 3 for x. One input with two outputs → not a function.

rememberIt.hook: Relation = any pairing; function = each input has exactly ONE output
rememberIt.unpack: Domain = inputs, range = outputs. One-one and onto describe how inputs map to outputs.

studentQuestions.a: A relation just pairs things up; a FUNCTION is a relation where every input gives exactly one output.
Domain = all allowed inputs; range = all outputs the function actually produces.
